paragraphs: Mocambo, sold alongside cacao, is Theobroma bicolor, a close relative of the chocolate tree (Theobroma cacao) rather than the same species. Native to Central and South America, including the Amazon basin of Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador and Peru, it goes by many names including jaguar tree, macambo and pataxte. Its seeds are edible, high in calories, and rich in protein and fibre, and like true cacao they can be processed into chocolate-like drinks.

Mocambo carries deep Mesoamerican history. It was cultivated by the Aztecs alongside cacao, and the Maya valued it highly: the beans make an unusually thick, stable foam, and the tree appears in the Popol Vuh, the Maya creation text, paired with cacao. Pataxte drinks are lower in caffeine than chocolate, and the beans are still prepared today for their prized foaming quality, with some artisan chocolate makers reviving its use.

claims.text: Mocambo sold as cacao is Theobroma bicolor, a relative of the chocolate tree Theobroma cacao but a distinct species.
It is native to Central and South America, including the Amazon rainforest of Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador and Peru, and is also called jaguar tree, macambo and pataxte.
Its seeds are edible, high in calories, and rich in protein and fibre, and can be used like cacao for chocolate-like drinks.
It was cultivated by the Aztecs alongside cacao and the beans produce an unusually thick, stable foam.
Pataxte appears in the Maya Popol Vuh paired with cacao, is lower in caffeine than chocolate, and is still prepared for its prized foaming quality.

sources.name: Chocolate Class (Harvard): Pataxte, Cacao's Cast-out Cousin

paragraphs: Trinitario is not a single clone but a recognised hybrid group of cacao, arising from natural crosses between Criollo and Forastero types. It is named after Trinidad, where it emerged in the 18th century after disease devastated the island's prized Criollo trees and growers replanted with hardier Forastero stock. The surviving Criollo and the introduced Forastero crossed, producing a population that was more disease resistant than Criollo while tasting better than the workhorse Forastero.

Because it combines fine flavour with vigour and resistance, Trinitario is widely grown as a fine, or flavour, cacao and prized by chocolate makers. Its beans tend toward balanced, fruity and floral aromatics rather than the flat, plain profile of bulk Forastero cocoa. Any cross between Criollo and Forastero can fall under the Trinitario label, so trees and their beans vary considerably from one selection to the next.

claims.text: Trinitario is a hybrid group of cacao derived from crosses between Criollo and Forastero, not a single clone.
It is named after Trinidad, where it emerged after disease destroyed the island's Criollo and growers replanted with Forastero, the two then crossing naturally.
Trinitario was more disease resistant than Criollo while tasting better than Forastero.
It is classed as a fine, or flavour, cacao and valued by chocolate makers for fruity and floral aromatics.

sources.name: Tropical Cacao: Understanding Cacao Varieties (Criollo, Forastero, Trinitario)
Chocolats du Monde: Trinitario cocoa, fusion of Criollo and Forastero
